questions.scenario: You need to write a multi-line config file to /tmp/reactor.conf from a script without creating a separate template file.
questions.question: Write a here-doc that creates /tmp/reactor.conf with two lines: 'FACILITY=reactor-a' and 'STATUS=online'.
questions.description: A here-doc (<<EOF...EOF) lets you write multi-line text inline in a script. The content is fed to the command's stdin. Variable expansion happens by default — use <<'EOF' to suppress it.
use_cases: Generate config files in deploy scripts
Create template files without separate template files
hints: Syntax: command <<EOF ... EOF on its own line
cat with a here-doc writes the text

questions.scenario: You need to write a here-doc that contains a literal $HOSTNAME without it being expanded to the actual hostname.
questions.question: Write a here-doc to /tmp/literal.txt containing the literal text '$HOSTNAME' (not the expanded value).
questions.description: Quoting the delimiter (<<'EOF') disables all variable expansion and command substitution inside the here-doc. The content is treated as a raw string. Essential when generating scripts or configs that contain shell variables.
use_cases: Generate shell scripts with variables meant for later execution
Write configs containing $ characters without escaping
Template files where variables should not be expanded at write time
hints: Quote the opening delimiter with single quotes: <<'EOF'

questions.scenario: You need to count words in a known string inline — no files, no pipes.
questions.question: Use a here-string to pass 'five four three two one' to wc and count its words.
questions.description: The here-string (<<<) feeds a single string to a command's stdin. It's lighter than a here-doc and avoids echo | command, which spawns a subshell. Bash-specific, not POSIX sh.
hints: The operator is <<< with the string after it
Run: wc -w <<< 'five four three two one'

questions.scenario: A script writes a SQL query inline using a here-doc. The USER variable should expand inside it.
questions.question: Use a here-doc to print a two-line message where the first line says 'Operator: ' followed by your actual $USER value.
questions.description: By default (unquoted delimiter), variables inside a here-doc ARE expanded. This lets you generate dynamic content based on current environment variables — the most common here-doc use case.
use_cases: Generate dynamic config with environment values
Build SQL queries with runtime-determined parameters
hints: Use <<EOF (no quotes) to allow variable expansion

Run: cat <<EOF
Operator: $USER
Status: online
EOF
